coredata.dc:title: Dopamine-functionalized upconversion nanoparticles as fluorescent sensors for organophosphorus pesticide analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Mengli
Wei, Junyi
Wang, Yao
Ouyang, Hui
Fu, Zhifeng
coredata.dc:description: Organophosphorus pesticide (OP) residues in agricultural products, herbal medicines and environment have attracted increasing concerns because they cause high healthy risk. Herein, a tyrosinase-mediated photoinduced electron transfer system was constructed for OPs analysis by using dopamine-functionalized upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) as fluorescent (FL) sensors. Dopamine quinone was produced by tyrosinase-mediated oxidation of dopamine on the surface of UCNPs, which acted as electron accepter to quench the FL emission of UCNPs. The FL quenching was inhibited by OP since it inhibited the activity of tyrosinase. Chlorpyrifos was used as a model analyte to investigate the feasibility of the FL sensor for the analysis of OPs. Under the optimal conditions, chlorpyrifos can be analysed in a wide range of 1.0 ‒ 1000 ng mL−1, with a detection limit of 0.38 ng mL−1 (3σ). Some other groups pesticides, including organonitrogen pesticide, organochlorine pesticide and chloronicotinyl insecticide all showed negligible interference. The proposed sensor was successfully used to analyse chlorpyrifos spiked in Balloonflower and Angelica with acceptable recovery values of 95.4–120.0%, demonstrating its application potential for real samples. It exhibits some advantages like low cost, high sensitivity and free of autofluorescent interference and photobleaching.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Upconversion nanoparticles
Organophosphorus pesticide
Chlorpyrifos
Fluorescent sensor
Tyrosinase
Photoinduced electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for the analysis of chlorpyrifos
Principle illustration of tyrosinase-mediated PET method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Preparation of UCNPs-DA. (B) Schematic illustration of the tyrosinase-mediated PET method for OPs analysis.

(A) TEM image of UCNPs-DA. (B) X-ray diffraction pattern of UCNPs-DA. (C) FL emission spectrum of UCNPs-DA. (D) XPS survey spectrum of UCNPs-DA. (E) C 1s XPS spectrum of UCNPs-DA. (F) N 1s XPS spectrum of UCNPs-DA.

FL emission spectra of (a) UCNPs-DA, (b) UCNPs-DA/tyrosinase/chlorpyrifos system and (c) UCNPs-DA/tyrosinase system. The concentrations of UCNPs-DA, tyrosinase and chlorpyrifos were 0.8 mg mL−1, 250 U mL−1 and 500 ng mL−1, respectively.

(A) Effect of the concentration of tyrosinase on the F/F
o ratio for chlorpyrifos analysis. (B) Effect of the pH value on the F/F
o ratio for chlorpyrifos analysis. (C) Effect of the incubation time on the F/F
o ratio for chlorpyrifos analysis. F and F
o represent the FL responses of chlorpyrifos at 10 ng mL−1 and 0, respectively. All other conditions were the chosen optimal conditions, n = 3.

FL spectra of UCNPs-DA in the presence of tyrosinase at 250 U mL−1 and chlorpyrifos at the concentrations of 0, 1, 10, 50, 100, 250, 500 and 1000 ng mL−1. (B) Calibration curve for chlorpyrifos analysis. All conditions were the chosen optimal conditions, n = 3.

F/F
o ratio of the UCNPs-DA/tyrosinase system for different pesticides. F
o and F represent the FL responses in the absence and presence of pesticides, respectively. (B) F′/F
o
' ratio of the UCNPs-DA/tyrosinase/chlorpyrifos system for the matrix substances. F
o
' and F′ represent the FL responses in the absence and presence of matrix substances, respectively. The concentration of all pesticides and GOx was 250 ng mL−1, and the concentration of others was 50-fold of the concentration of pesticides. All other conditions were the chosen optimal conditions, n = 3.
Recovery tests of chlorpyrifos spiked in Balloonflower and Angelica samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Dopamine-functionalized upconversion nanoparticles as fluorescent sensors for organophosphorus pesticide analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Ministry of Education), College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400716, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Ministry of Education), College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organophosphorus pesticide (OP) residues in agricultural products, herbal medicines and environment have attracted increasing concerns because they cause high healthy risk. Herein, a tyrosinase-mediated photoinduced electron transfer system was constructed for OPs analysis by using dopamine-functionalized upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) as fluorescent (FL) sensors. Dopamine quinone was produced by tyrosinase-mediated oxidation of dopamine on the surface of UCNPs, which acted as electron accepter to quench the FL emission of UCNPs. The FL quenching was inhibited by OP since it inhibited the activity of tyrosinase. Chlorpyrifos was used as a model analyte to investigate the feasibility of the FL sensor for the analysis of OPs. Under the optimal conditions, chlorpyrifos can be analysed in a wide range of 1.0 ‒ 1000 ng mL−1, with a detection limit of 0.38 ng mL−1 (3σ). Some other groups pesticides, including organonitrogen pesticide, organochlorine pesticide and chloronicotinyl insecticide all showed negligible interference. The proposed sensor was successfully used to analyse chlorpyrifos spiked in Balloonflower and Angelica with acceptable recovery values of 95.4–120.0%, demonstrating its application potential for real samples. It exhibits some advantages like low cost, high sensitivity and free of autofluorescent interference and photobleaching.

A tyrosinase-mediated photoinduced electron transfer method was constructed for fluorescent analysis of organophosphorus pesticide by using dopamine-functionalized upconversion nanoparticles.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) have been widely used in modern agriculture due to their high insecticidal efficiency [1,2]. However, they are neurotoxic due to their inhibition to the activity of acetylcholinesterase in the central and peripheral nervous system [3], which raises public concerns about human health. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop sensitive, rapid and low-cost methods for analysing OP residues in agricultural products, herbal medicines and environmental samples.

Up to now, multiple analytical methods have been used for the analysis of OPs, including HPLC [4], HPLC/GC-MS [5,6] and GC-ion mobility spectrometric analysis [7]. Although the above-mentioned methods are sensitive and reliable, there still exist some disadvantages such as sophisticated instruments, tedious sample preparation, high analytical cost and time-consuming analysis procedure, which restrict their application for rapid screening. To overcome these disadvantages, some immunoassay methods such as colorimetric immunoassay [8], fluorimetric immunoassay [9] and electrochemical immunoassay [10] have been developed. However, these immunoassay-based methods suffer from very high cost of antibodies. Meanwhile the tedious signal probe labeling of antibodies sometimes leads to loss of their activity. Therefore, some enzymatic biosensors have been reported for sensitive analysis of OPs, which include electrochemical biosensor [11,12], electrochemiluminescent biosensor [13,14] and colorimetric assay [15]. Among them electrochemical and electrochemiluminescent biosensors sometimes suffer from poor reproducibility and ease to be interfered by complicated sample matrix. Colorimetric assays always show insufficient sensitivity, which might lead to false-negative results. Recently, fluorescent (FL) sensors based on functional materials and enzyme inhibition have been reported for the analysis of OPs [16,17]. These FL methods show high sensitivity and facile manipulation. Their main drawbacks are photobleaching of FL probes and high background from autofluorescence of biological sample matrixes.

Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) are lanthanide-doped inorganic materials, which are capable of converting near-infrared excitation to UV or visible emission via sequentially absorbing two or more low-energy photons [18]. In comparison with traditional FL probes such as organic dye and FL protein, UCNPs possess unique physicochemical features including large anti-Stokes shifts, negligible photobleaching and long-term chemical stability. Under near-infrared excitation, UCNPs are free of autofluorescence interference from various sample matrixes, which provides low background signal and high signal-to-noise ratio [19,20]. Hence, UCNPs are regarded as promising alternatives for traditional FL probes and used in cyto labeling [21–23], bioimaging [24–28] and biosensing [29–33]. In these works, UCNPs are usually adopted to trace biorecognition events of antibody, nucleic acid and cell. However, there are few reports concerning small molecules-functionalized UCNPs for biosensing. Recently, UCNPs have also been reported to serve as electron donors to construct photoinduced electron transfer (PET) systems. Upon near-infrared excitation, the excited photoelectrons of UCNPs can be captured by electron acceptors, such as quinones and melanin-like polymers. The PET processes usually lead to quenching of the fluorescent emission of UCNPs [34].

Herein, novel dopamine-functionalized UCNPs (UCNPs-DA) were synthesized and used as FL sensors for OPs analysis. A tyrosinase-mediated PET process occurring on the sensors led to FL quenching of UCNPs. OPs inhibited the activity of tyrosinase and the PET process, which allowed sensitive analysis of OPs. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report focusing on UCNPs-DA and their application in biosensing up to now.

Yttrium(III) chloride hexahydrate (YCl3·6H2O), ytterbium(III) chloride hexahydrate (YbCl3·6H2O), thulium(III) chloride hexahydrate (TmCl3·6H2O), ammonlum fluoride (NH4F), chlorpyrifos, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid monohydrate (MES), oleic acid, 1-octadecene, glucose, glycine, histidine, serine and glucose oxidase (GOx) from Aspergillus niger were all provided by Aladdin Reagent Ltd. (China). Dopamine was obtained from J&K Chemical Ltd. (China). Poly (acrylic acid) (PAA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Ltd. (USA). Tyrosinase was provided by Solarbio Ltd. (China). QuEChERS clean-up kit composed of primary secondary amine sorbent, octadecylsilane sorbent, graphitized carbon black sorbent and MgSO4 was obtained from Biocomma Ltd. (China). Balloonflower and Angelica powder were purchased from local pharmacy and confirmed by GC-MS to be free of chlorpyrifos. Polystyrene 96-well black microplates were purchased from Corning Ltd. (USA).

FL spectra were measured using a F-7000 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi Ltd., Japan). An external 980-nm laser diode (Hi-Tech Optoelectronic Co., Ltd., China) was utilized as the excitation source. The crystalline structure of UCNPs was characterized by a X′Pert³ powder diffractometer (PANalytical Ltd., the Netherland). TEM image of UCNPs was obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI Ltd., USA). XPS analysis was conducted using an ESCALAB 250 Xi XPS spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd., USA). GC-MS analysis was conducted on an Agilent 7890B GC system equipped with 5977B GC/MSD (Agilent Ltd., USA)

NaYF4:Yb,Tm UCNPs were synthesized by a modified thermal decomposition method. In brief, YCl3·6H2O (237 mg), YbCl3·6H2O (78 mg) and TmCl3·6H2O (7.7 mg) were mixed with 6.0 mL of oleic acid and 14 mL of 1-octadecene in a three-neck flask. The mixture was heated to 160 °C with continuous stirring for 40 min under atmosphere of argon. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature (RT), it was slowly added with 10 mL of methanol containing 10 mg of NaOH and 148 mg of NH4F, and stirred for 30 min. To remove methanol and water, the product was heated to 100 °C for 30 min under constant stirring. Subsequently, the mixed solution was heated to 300 °C and maintained for 1 h. After cooling to RT, the UCNPs were precipitated by adding 20 mL of ethanol, and then washed thrice using 30 mL of ethanol/cyclohexane (2:1, v/v). Following centrifugal separation, the product was dried overnight at 70 °C in a vacuum oven to obtain dry powder of NaYF4:Yb,Tm UCNPs.

PAA-coated NaYF4:Yb,Tm UCNPs (UCNPs-PAA) were prepared by ligand exchange [35]. In brief, 30 mg of UCNPs dispersed in 4.0 mL of chloroform was slowly added into 8.0 mL of PAA aqueous solution at 25 mg mL−1, followed by 24-h stirring at 37 °C. UCNPs-PAA dispersed in the upper water layer were collected and washed thrice using water. Lastly, the solution was freeze dried overnight to obtain dry powder of UCNPs-PAA.

Dopamine was covalently conjugated to UCNPs-PAA using an EDC/NHS amidization reaction. In brief, 4.0 mg of UCNPs-PAA were activated by 1.5 mL of MES buffer (0.10 M, pH 6.5) containing 8.0 mg of EDC and the same amount of NHS at RT for 15 min. Afterward, 500 μL of dopamine solution at 16 mg mL−1 was added to react at RT for 1 h. After thrice centrifugal washing, the obtained UCNPs-DA were dispersed into 5.0 mL of phosphate buffered saline (PBS) at 0.10 M and pH 7.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the analysis of chlorpyrifos
ce:section.ce:para: Seventy five microliters of chlorpyrifos sample was mixed with 150 μL of UCNPs-DA at 0.8 mg mL−1 and 75 μL of tyrosinase solution at 250 U mL−1. After 1-h incubation at 37 °C, the FL signal at 475 nm was collected with excitation at 980 nm.

The herbal medicine samples were treated with QuEChERS clean-up kit. In brief, 0.5 g of dried Balloonflower or Angelica powder was mixed thoroughly with known concentrations of chlorpyrifos for 15 min. Then 10 mL of 10% methanol was added into the spiked samples and vortex mixed for 3 min to extract chlorpyrifos. After centrifugal separation, the supernatant was collected and mixed with the clean-up agents of the QuEChERS kit. After 1-min vortex and centrifugal separation, the supernatant was collected and filtered through a filter membrane with a pore size of 0.22 µm. Finally, the solution was diluted with PBS to 50 mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle illustration of tyrosinase-mediated PET method
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1, UCNPs-DA were prepared and utilized as FL sensors for sensitive analysis of OPs based on a tyrosinase-mediated PET mechanism. Dopamine quinone was produced by the oxidation of dopamine in the presence of tyrosinase. Dopamine quinone on the surface of UCNPs efficiently quenched the FL emission of UCNPs via a PET process. When OPs existed in the UCNPs-DA/tyrosinase system, less dopamine quinone was produced due to the inhibited activity of tyrosinase. Thus, stronger FL emission could be observed in the presence of OPs. In this proof-of-principle work, chlorpyrifos was analysed as a model analyte.

NaYF4:Yb,Tm UCNPs were synthesized via a thermal decomposition method, and coated by PAA via ligand exchange. The PAA-coated NaYF4:Yb,Tm UCNPs were then functionalized via a EDC/ NHS amidization reaction to prepare UCNPs-DA (Fig. 1A). As shown in Fig. 2A, the prepared UCNPs-DA was ellipsoidal and well dispersed, and the mean length and width were about 36 nm and 28 nm, respectively. The X-ray diffraction analysis in Fig. 2B reveals that UCNPs-DA were hexagonal phase (JCPDS no. 27-0814), which contributed to their high FL efficiency. Fig. 2C displays a typical FL spectrum of UCNPs-DA with 980 nm laser excitation, which showed negligible difference with that of un-functionalized UCNPs. Four main emission peaks were recorded at 345, 361, 450 and 475 nm. The strongest emission at 475 nm was attributed to the 1G4–3H6 transition of Tm3+ [36], which was utilized in the following analysis application. The chemical composition of UCNPs-DA was investigated by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2D, Na, Tm, Yb, Y, F, O, C and N elements were found in the nanoparticles. Fig. 2E displayed two major peaks of C 1s at 285.0 and 288.6 eV, for which the fitted peaks at 284.5, 285.0, 285.7 and 288.6 eV corresponded to C˭C, C‒C, C‒N (C‒O) and C˭O groups. Moreover, the characteristic peak of N 1s at 399.9 eV (Fig. 2F) confirmed the formation of UCNPs-DA.

As previously reported, dopamine quinone was the oxidation product of dopamine in the presence of tyrosinase, which can act as an efficient electron acceptor to quench the FL of carbon quantum dots [37,38], quantum dots [39,40] and AuNPs [41] via PET. Furthermore, it is well known that the activity of tyrosinase can be efficiently inhibited by OPs [42,43]. As seen in Fig. 3, UCNPs-DA showed a strong FL emission at 475 nm (blue). For UCNPs-DA at 0.4 mg mL−1, the relative standard deviation (RSD) value of FL signals was 6.2%. After dopamine was oxidized to dopamine quinone in the presence of tyrosinase, the FL emission was greatly inhibited (red). When chlorpyrifos was added into the UCNPs-DA/tyrosinase system, UCNPs emitted much stronger FL signal (black) in comparison with the system without chlorpyrifos. The above results proved the feasibility of the proposed tyrosinase-mediated PET method.

To obtain ideal analytical performance, some conditions including the concentration of tyrosinase, the pH value and the incubation time were optimized by using chlorpyrifos at 10 ng mL−1. In the study, the F/F
o ratio was used as a standard to select the optimal condition, where F
o and F represent the FL responses of chlorpyrifos at 0 and 10 ng mL−1, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 4A and S1, the F/F
o ratio reached a maximum value at 250 U mL−1 tyrosinase, thus this concentration was chosen in the following experiments. Meanwhile, the pH value affected both the activity of tyrosinase and the stability of chlorpyrifos. As shown in Fig. 4B and S2, the F/F
o ratio reached maximum in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.4, thus the buffer was adopted in the following experiments. Another critical factor influencing the FL response was the incubation time. After careful investigation, the incubation time was chosen to be 60 min since it provided the strongest response (Fig. 4C and S3).

Under the optimal conditions, the FL quenching of UCNPs-DA was gradually inhibited with increasing concentration of chlorpyrifos since the activity of tyrosinase was inhibited by chlorpyrifos ( Fig. 5A). Inhibition efficiency (IE %) was calculated as the following equation:
(1)
IE (%)
=
(
F
c
−
F
o
)
/
(
F
t
−
F
o
)
×
100
where F
t , F
o , F
c represent the FL intensities of UCNPs-DA, UCNPs-DA/tyrosinase system and UCNPs-DA/tyrosinase/chlorpyrifos system, respectively. Fig. 5B shows the curvilinear relationship between IE (%) and the concentration of chlorpyrifos in a wide range from 1.0 to 1000 ng mL−1. The limit of detection was estimated to be 0.38 ng mL−1 at 3σ, which is lower than most of the previously reported works, such as GC, HPLC, surface-enhanced Raman scattering spectroscopy and electrochemical biosensor (Table S1). The regression equation was described as IE (%) = 94.888−83.738
×
e-C/923.045 (R
2 = 0.995), where C represents the concentration of chlorpyrifos (ng mL−1). RSD values of FL signals were measured for chlorpyrifos at low (10 ng mL−1), medium (100 ng mL−1) and high (500 ng mL−1) concentrations. The obtained RSD values for low, medium and high concentrations were 8.5%, 5.4% and 6.4%, respectively (n = 3). The results demonstrated that the tyrosinase-mediated PET method using UCNPs-DA was highly sensitive for the analysis of chlorpyrifos with satisfying reproducibility.

To further evaluate the selectivity of the method for the analysis of OPs, the potential interference of some other groups pesticides was investigated, including organonitrogen pesticide (monuron and diuron), organochlorine pesticide (chlorothalonil) and chloronicotinyl insecticide (thiacloprid). In the selectivity investigation, the concentration of all pesticides was 250 ng mL−1. The degree of interference (DI) of these potential interferents was calculated according to the following equation:
(2)
DI
=
( A −
C
)
/
(
B
−
C
)
×
100%
where A, B and C are the FL intensities originated from the interferents, chlorpyrifos and blank sample (PBS), respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 6A, the DI values of the four pesticides were all lower than 7.6%, showing negligible interference. Besides, some common substances coexisted in plant were also tested to evaluate the matrix interference (MI) of the method. K+, Mg2+, Na+, Cu2+, glucose, glycine, histidine, serine and GOx were used as the matrix substances in plant. The MI values were calculated as the following equation:
(3)
MI
=
(
E
−
D
)
/
D
×
100%
where D and E are the FL intensities originated from UCNPs-DA/tyrosinase/chlorpyrifos system in the absence and presence of the matrix substances, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6B, the MI values ranged from −4.40% to 8.7%, indicating that the method can exclude interference from common matrix substance existing in plant samples.

Two herbal medicines including Balloonflower and Angelica were used as real samples to evaluate the application potential of the method. The samples were confirmed by GC-MS to be free of chlorpyrifos. They were spiked with chlorpyrifos at low (10 ng mL−1), medium (100 ng mL−1) and high (500 ng mL−1) concentrations, and then analysed with the tyrosinase-mediated PET-based method. As shown in Table 1, the obtained results exhibit acceptable recovery values ranging from 95.4% to 120.0% with RSD values all below 8.5%, indicating the reliability of the method for analysing chlorpyrifos in real samples.

In summary, novel UCNPs-DA were synthesized and used as FL sensors for OPs analysis. Then a tyrosinase-mediated PET-based method was established for sensitive analysis of chlorpyrifos using UCNPs as the electron donors and dopamine quinone as the electron acceptor. The method was established based on the activity inhibition of tyrosinase by OPs, in which chlorpyrifos was utilized as a model analyte. Other OPs can also be analysed by using the same UCNPs-DA since the method is a universal platform. Superior to traditional FL probes, UCNPs exhibit large anti-stokes shift and high photostability. Therefore, the method was free of autofluorescent interference and photobleaching. It also exhibits some other advantages like low cost, high sensitivity and satisfying reproducibility. The successful application in herbal medicines analysis indicates its application potential for real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775125), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2018jcyjAX0175) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2017A008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.11.105.
